title: NICE 2025 Falls Guidance: What It Means for Falls Prevention
description: NICE updated its falls prevention guidelines in 2025. Here is what changed, what it means for operators and clinicians, and how Able Assess aligns with the new guidance.
author: Able Care Clinical Team
excerpt: NICE updated its falls prevention guidelines in 2025, reinforcing the case for objective, standardized, multifactorial screening. Here is what the guidance says, what changed and what it means for operators, clinicians and commissioners.
content:
What NICE 2025 says

The National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) updated its falls prevention guidelines in 2025. The updated guidance reinforces several principles that have been central to Able Care's approach from the beginning.

Multifactorial assessment. NICE recommends that all older adults identified as being at risk of falls should receive a multifactorial falls risk assessment. This means assessing multiple domains of function, not relying on a single test or a clinician's subjective judgment.

Standardized protocols. The guidance emphasizes the importance of standardized, validated assessment tools that deliver consistent results regardless of who administers them or where the assessment takes place.

Objective measurement. NICE recommends objective measurement of physical function, including gait, balance and muscle strength. This moves away from subjective observation-based scoring toward quantifiable, comparable data.

What changed from previous guidance

The 2025 update strengthens the language around three areas.

Technology-enabled assessment. The updated guidance explicitly acknowledges the role of digital tools in delivering standardized, objective screening at scale. Previous versions focused on clinical settings and trained assessors. The 2025 update recognizes that non-clinical staff using validated digital tools can deliver screening that meets the clinical standard.

Population-level screening. NICE now emphasizes the value of systematic screening across populations, not just reactive assessment after a fall or near-miss. This aligns with the population health approach that multi-site operators need.

Data and outcomes tracking. The guidance strengthens recommendations around longitudinal tracking of functional status and falls outcomes. Digital tools that capture and trend data over time are better positioned to meet this requirement than paper-based methods.

How Able Assess aligns

Able Assess was designed from the ground up to meet the standard that NICE 2025 now recommends.

Multifactorial. Four validated metrics (grip strength, sit-to-stand, gait speed, Timed Up and Go) captured in a single workflow.

Standardized. One protocol across every branch, community or clinical site. The same result regardless of who runs the screening.

Objective. Sensor-based measurement scored against normative data. No subjective observation.

Scalable. Usable by non-clinical staff with minimal training. Deployable across multi-site operations in under 30 days.

Data-driven. Automatic digital capture with longitudinal trending, population-level dashboards and integration with care plans and EHR systems.

What this means for operators and commissioners

For UK operators, alignment with NICE 2025 is increasingly a commissioning requirement. Demonstrating that your falls prevention approach meets the updated guidance strengthens your position with commissioners, CQC and local authorities.

For US operators, the NICE 2025 guidance reflects the same evidence base that underpins CDC best practice and HHVBP measures. The principles are the same: objective, standardized, multifactorial screening protects outcomes and reduces preventable hospitalizations.

Key takeaways

  • NICE 2025 reinforces the case for objective, standardized, multifactorial falls risk screening.
  • The updated guidance explicitly acknowledges technology-enabled assessment by non-clinical staff.
  • Population-level screening and longitudinal tracking are now stronger recommendations.
  • Able Assess aligns with every pillar of the NICE 2025 guidance: multifactorial, standardized, objective, scalable and data-driven.
  • Alignment with NICE 2025 strengthens commissioning and regulatory positioning for UK operators.
